entities.name: śakti
entities.cite: The third item, on the power of words, is the one that matters for his own project

entities.name: yataśakti
entities.cite: read as yata- ('restrained, fixed') it yields the determinate or fixed power of words

entities.name: āṅgatā
entities.cite: Angata is the technical Mimamsa relation of subordination — a subsidiary element standing to a principal rite

axioms.name: asti_sakti
axioms.cite: The third item, on the power of words, is the one that matters for his own project
read as yata- ('restrained, fixed') it yields the determinate or fixed power of words

denials.name: aniyata
denials.cite: the compound is awkward and the line resists a confident construal on the karika alone

rejected_readings.label: indeterminate_power
rejected_readings.rendering: Words possess an indeterminate and variable power that manifests differently across ritual traditions.
rejected_readings.why: The verse and commentary emphasize yata-saktitvam as determinate and fixed, not variable. This reading contradicts the core assertion that power is restrained and fixed.

rejected_readings.label: power_as_property
rejected_readings.rendering: Words have a variable property that depends on context rather than an intrinsic determinate power.
rejected_readings.why: Demotes the power of words to a contextual property. The axiom licenses sakti (sort=power) as identical with yatasakti, but this mistaken reading treats sakti as a mere property, creating a sort mismatch that leaves the identity unlicensed.

notes: Verse 1.6 is metacommentary on textual recensions, asserting that observation of ritual and textual variants reveals the determinate power of words. The 'angata' relation provides a Mimamsa context but is not the focus of the formalization; the core claim is that words possess yata-sakti (determinate power), demonstrable across traditions.
